Content:
Customer health scores are the astrology of customer success.

Both promise to predict the future using complex systems. Both give believers a sense of control over uncertain outcomes. Both consistently fail when it matters most.

The difference? When your horoscope is wrong, you lose nothing. When your health score is wrong, you lose customers.

94.8% of CS teams now use AI-powered health scoring. Platforms compete on algorithm sophistication. Leaders present colored dashboards as proof of predictive mastery.

Customers still churn without warning.

Your highest-value enterprise client scores 98% healthy. Usage is up. Support tickets down. Then they submit a 90-day cancellation notice.

The algorithm missed what a 15-minute phone call would have revealed: they've been evaluating competitors for six months.

Your CSMs already know which accounts are at risk. Trust their judgment over the algorithm's confidence.

I break down why health scores actively make teams worse at retention: https://churnisdead.com/newsletter/case-against-customer-health-scores

Content:
False positives in health scoring waste more resources than false negatives.

Mid-market accounts with 60% health scores get aggressive intervention campaigns. CSMs run discovery calls to identify expansion opportunities. Account managers get pulled into strategic conversations.

Meanwhile, the account was never at risk. They're seasonal users who go quiet every Q4. The health score algorithm doesn't know their business cycle.

False negatives create blind spots, but false positives create something worse: resource misallocation at scale.

When your team optimizes for metrics instead of relationships, three things happen:
• You waste time on healthy accounts
• You miss context that actually matters
• You train CSMs to game the system instead of serve customers

The best CSMs I know ignore the dashboard colors. They focus on conversation quality, stakeholder mapping, and business outcome alignment.

What do you rely on more: your health score or your gut feeling about an account?

Content:
4 relationship triggers that matter more than health scores:

1. **Champion transition alerts**
Your internal advocate changes roles or companies. This matters more than any usage metric.

2. **Stakeholder mapping gaps**
You're only connected to one person in a multi-stakeholder decision. Relationship depth beats engagement frequency.

3. **Business outcome misalignment**
They're using your product, but not achieving their stated goals. Usage stats won't catch this disconnect.

4. **Expansion readiness signals**
Identifying growth opportunities within existing usage patterns. This prevents churn by building deeper partnerships.

These triggers focus on relationship strength rather than risk mitigation. They prevent churn by building stronger partnerships that survive contextual changes.

Health scores tell you everything except what you need to know.

Content:
Your QBR format encourages performance theater instead of business decisions.

Traditional script: Health scores by segment. NRR trends. Win/loss analysis. Customer feedback themes. Action items for next quarter.

Teams spend weeks crafting narratives explaining why metrics moved. Building compelling explanations for trailing indicators while leading indicators go unexamined.

Executives nod along, ask clarifying questions about historical performance, then leave without making any forward-looking resource allocation decisions.

Meanwhile, the obstacles that will determine next quarter's results remain unaddressed. The resource gaps that will create churn stay unfilled. The strategic initiatives that could drive expansion never get prioritized.

The highest-leverage meeting in your CS organization becomes the least impactful.

Instead of asking 'What happened last quarter?' start asking 'What decisions do we need to make this month?'

Instead of reviewing trailing health scores, identify leading obstacle patterns.

Instead of explaining past performance, commit resources to future outcomes.

What's the biggest obstacle your customers face that your executive team could actually remove?

Most CS teams celebrate 112% NRR. But break it down:

• 88% came from 2 enterprise accounts expanding regardless
• CS team of 12 managed routine check-ins for 400+ accounts
• Those 400 accounts renewed at exactly contract value
• Net CS-influenced growth: 2%
• CS cost as % of revenue: 8%

That's not sustainable math.

Your CFO sees this breakdown. You report the headline number.

The disconnect isn't that CFOs don't value customers. It's that CS metrics don't translate to financial impact.

What would change if you measured revenue-weighted retention instead of account-weighted retention?

Content:
3 financial lenses your CFO uses to evaluate CS:

1. Unit Economics Reality
What's the fully-loaded cost to retain $1 of ARR? Include salary, benefits, tools, office space, management overhead, recruiting, training, and opportunity cost. Most CS teams think $0.15-0.25. Reality is $0.40-0.65.

2. Investment Payback Timeline
How long does CS headcount take to pay for itself? Not impact on retention rates, but when prevented churn revenue exceeds total cost of hire. CS leaders think quarterly. CFOs think 18-24 month payback periods.

3. Opportunity Cost Analysis
Every CS dollar is a dollar not spent on product, sales, or marketing. Would this CS investment generate more revenue as two AEs instead?

You see CS as essential. They see it as one option among many.

Content:
Enterprise customers aren't paying you $200K to feel good about poor adoption.

They're paying you to ensure they get $2M in value.

Those are different jobs.

CS teams have confused being customer-centric with never making customers uncomfortable. So we create elaborate justification frameworks for why their ROI calculation is wrong, why their usage patterns are actually fine, and why their concerns aren't valid.

Meanwhile, their business metrics deteriorate. Their executive sponsor gets fired. Their budget gets cut. And they blame the economy.

The enterprise reality: Your customer's comfort today determines your renewal tomorrow.

But not in the way you think.

Comfortable conversations about uncomfortable metrics lead to churn. Uncomfortable conversations about comfortable metrics lead to expansion.

What uncomfortable conversation are you avoiding with your biggest customer?

Content:
Three forces turned strategic advisors into professional excuse-makers:

1. The Support Team Mindset Migration
When companies moved support teams into CS, they imported the core principle: resolve immediate concerns with minimal escalation. Support optimizes for case closure. CS adopted this for conversation closure.

2. The Vendor-Customer Power Dynamic Myth
CS leaders teach that customers hold all the power in renewals. This creates a negotiating stance where CS argues from weakness instead of value.

3. The Metrics Misalignment
We measure customer satisfaction instead of customer progress. We celebrate CSMs who manage difficult conversations well instead of CSMs who eliminate the need for them.

The result: CS teams that predict churn perfectly but prevent it poorly.

The antidote: Courage-based conversations that focus on outcomes, not comfort.

Content:
An AI VP of Customer Success emailing 83 sponsors at 12:20am with custom check-ins. No human in the loop. That was his proof.

He's right about the work. He's wrong about the conclusion.

The role doesn't die. It splits.

It splits into two jobs almost nobody was hired to do. The supervisor who stops the agent from sending a tone-deaf email to a grieving champion. The closer who navigates a procurement freeze the bot can't touch.

The administrative core was 60 to 70 percent of the CSM week. That part is machine work now. Fine. It was always going to be.

But here's the uncomfortable part. Most CSMs hired in the 2021 boom were hired for the layer the agent just absorbed. Relationship building. Being proactive. Cadence in green on a dashboard.

If your CSM job could be done by an agent emailing 83 people at midnight, it was already a dead job. The agent didn't kill it. It made it visible.

Most teams are about to sleepwalk into the worse half of the split while leadership calls it strategic transformation.

I broke down both halves and the one number that tells you which one you're building.

Content:
Picture a real Fortune 500 account.

The renewal is a board-level budget fight. The champion just left for a competitor. Security flagged a data-residency issue that froze the expansion. Three internal stakeholders are quietly knifing each other over who owns the platform.

Now drop an AI agent on it.

It can send a beautiful, personalised, perfectly timed email to every stakeholder. It will do nothing.

Because that account doesn't need outreach. It needs judgment.

It needs someone to halt the send. To read the politics. To call the champion's old boss instead of emailing the new one. To know that the right move this week is silence.

The agents won the high-volume transactional work. They earned it. They are better than a tired human at midnight.

What they cannot do is the part we always mistook for admin. Reading a room. Holding a line in procurement. Knowing when not to act.

That was always the actual job. The midnight emails were just the part we confused for it.

So here's the question. What percentage of your week is judgment versus motion?

Strategy: Pure value with a vivid concrete scenario. No link. The closing question invites CSMs to self-assess and comment, driving engagement before the framework post lands.

Content:
Lemkin's AI agent killed the administrative CSM. What's left divides into two distinct human jobs.

Most teams will build the wrong one by accident.

The supervisor:

1. Watches the agents. Reviews what they send.
2. Catches the tone-deaf, the off-brand, the catastrophic.
3. Manages by exception. Scales across hundreds of accounts.
4. Lower leverage. Closer to QA than strategy.

The closer:

1. Owns the accounts agents cannot touch.
2. Navigates board fights, procurement freezes, champion churn.
3. Sits in the room where the renewal is actually decided.
4. Higher leverage. Closer to sales than support.

The trap:

Most 2021-boom CSMs were hired for neither. They were hired for the relationship layer the agent just absorbed. So when the split happens, they get sorted into the supervisor seat by default. Babysitting bots. Calling it transformation.

The number that decides it: your judgment ratio. The share of your week that requires reading a situation a machine cannot read.

Low ratio, you're a supervisor. High ratio, you're a closer.

Run the audit before the org chart runs it for you.

I carried 40 accounts. My Monday was an inbox of escalations, a sales handoff with no notes, two fire drills in Slack, and the one account threatening to leave that quarter.

I wrote check-in emails between meetings. I prepped QBR decks at 9pm. I updated the CRM on Friday so the forecast looked clean.

I called it proactive work. My dashboard showed my outreach cadence in green. I was proud of it.

Then I watched an AI agent do all of it. Better. At midnight. Without complaining.

And I had to sit with an ugly truth. The work I was proudest of was the work that was easiest to replace. The cadence. The check-ins. The clean CRM. All of it motion, almost none of it judgment.

The hard part of the job was the part I never had time for. The political read. The procurement fight. Knowing when not to send.

The agent didn't take my job. It exposed which half of it ever mattered.

Content:
Usage up. Tickets down. Last NPS a 9, written by a power user who'd built three workflows on top of us.

Then the renewal email arrived. Polite. Final.

"We've made a strategic decision to consolidate platforms."

The decision had been made eleven weeks earlier. In a budget meeting nobody from my side was in. By a CFO who'd joined four months prior and was clearing vendor spend for an AI initiative the board wanted funded.

None of that touched our telemetry. None of it could.

Here's the part nobody wants to sit with. The model didn't fail. The model did exactly what it was built to do. It measured product reality. And inside that frame, the account was healthy.

But renewals don't get decided inside your product. They get decided inside the customer's org chart, their budget cycle, and conversations you'll never be cc'd on.

I call this off-telemetry churn. The share of lost ARR your dashboard never flagged. Not flagged late. Never flagged.

The harder your vendor sells you 85% churn-prediction accuracy, the more you forget what that 85% is accurate about. It's accurate about the half of churn that lives in your data. It says nothing about the other half.

#CustomerSuccess #ChurnPrevention #SaaS
Strategy: Opens with the loss story that creates curiosity and stakes. The contrarian reframe (the model didn't fail) earns the click. Names the concept to make it memorable.

Content:
Most CS teams treat the dashboard as if it covers the whole renewal.

It doesn't. It covers product reality. People logging in. Features adopted. Support load. All real. All measurable.

But here's what your stack has zero sensors pointed at:

The budget meeting where a new CFO rationalizes the vendor stack.

The org reshuffle that retires your champion's role.

The private Slack where a peer recommends a competitor.

The board mandate that reallocates spend toward an AI initiative.

The strategic decision to consolidate platforms.

None of that crosses the boundary your instrumentation can reach. It was never built to.

So when a green account churns, the question everyone asks is "why didn't the model catch it." Wrong question. The model reported faithfully on the only thing it could see.

The better question: how much of what walked out your door was ever yours to catch in the first place?

That number tells you whether you're running customer success or just watching a very confident graph.

What's the most blindsided you've ever been by a renewal that looked green?

#CustomerSuccess #CustomerRetention #SaaS
Strategy: Pure value with no link. The list of off-telemetry signals is concrete and shareable. Ends with a question designed to surface war stories in comments.

Content:
Off-telemetry churn is the lost ARR your stack never flagged. Here's where it hides:

1. The budget cycle
A CFO rationalizing spend doesn't log in to tell you. The decision happens in a spreadsheet you'll never see.

2. The org chart
Your champion gets promoted, reorged, or replaced. The relationship that carried the account evaporates. Usage stays flat right up to the cancel.

3. The board mandate
A new strategic priority gets funded by clearing vendor spend. Your product becomes a line item to cut, not a tool to evaluate.

4. The peer conversation
Your buyer asks three people in their network what they use. You're not in that thread. You never will be.

5. The consolidation play
"We're standardizing on one platform." It's not about your product. That's exactly the problem.

None of these show up as a usage dip. None trigger a support spike. The health score stays green because the model is measuring the wrong territory.

The fix isn't a better model. It's admitting the dashboard is a map, not the territory, and building human coverage for the half no sensor reaches.

Full framework and a free audit in comments.

#CustomerSuccess #ChurnPrevention #CustomerRetention
Strategy: Numbered listicle is highly skimmable and saveable. Each item is a distinct, recognizable scenario CS leaders have lived. Link in comments protects reach.

Content:
I kept correcting them. The model didn't miss it.

The model reported faithfully on the only thing it could see. We missed it. Because we'd quietly decided the dashboard was the territory instead of a map of one quarter of it.

That's a harder thing to admit than "the tool failed."

Because the tool didn't fail. We did. We let a confident green health score do our thinking for us. We stopped asking the customer hard questions because the graph looked calm. We confused instrumentation with intimacy.

The account that taught me this had a power user who genuinely loved the product. Built three workflows on it. Gave us a 9. And it still walked, on ninety days notice, for reasons that lived entirely inside their org and never once touched our data.

The lesson stuck. A green dashboard is not a renewed account. It's a partial picture, confidently rendered.

So here's my challenge. Pull your last twelve months of losses. Sort them honestly. Find out how much was ever yours to catch.

The number you get back is the real measure of your CS function.

#CustomerSuccess #Leadership #SaaS
Strategy: Vulnerability angle. Admits the team's own failure rather than blaming tools, which builds credibility. The audit challenge makes it actionable. Subscribe CTA.

Content:
Median private SaaS NRR fell from 105% in 2021 to 101% in 2024.

Here is the part nobody says out loud: gross retention barely moved during that slide.

That changes who is responsible.

Gross retention holding flat means customers are not leaving. Net retention falling means they stopped buying more. Those are two completely different failures.

The second one is not yours.

Upsell stalled. Cross-sell stalled. Price increases stalled. CIOs are openly asking if they even need to renew at the same level. None of that is a value-realization problem you can fix in a QBR.

It is a pricing-power and roadmap problem.

Yet NRR still sits proudly on the goals slide of nearly every VP of CS I know. It was the number that finally got CS into the board deck.

Now that same number is sliding for reasons no CSM can touch. And the person who volunteered to own it is about to get asked why.

If you accept a raw NRR target this planning cycle, you have signed up to be blamed for a decline you could not have prevented.

I broke down how to stop that here: https://churnisdead.com/newsletter/expansion-recession-not-retention-problem

#CustomerSuccess #SaaS #NRR
Strategy: Hot take that reframes a number everyone obsesses over. Leads with a fear hook (you will be blamed) that CS leaders feel acutely during planning season.

Content:
Here is what a falling NRR actually looks like inside a real enterprise book.

Three types of accounts. All rolling into one blended number.

Type one: accounts that adopted everything you sold them. Usage healthy. Champion engaged. Renewal locked. They love you. They are also done. No new module, no new tier, no reason to spend another dollar. You did your job perfectly and the account is flat.

Type two: accounts that grew only because a contractual price escalator finally got enforced after years of being waived. The book grew. You ran no expansion play. The clause did the work.

Type three: a thin slice where something genuinely new landed. A second team picked up the product for a use case nobody scoped at signing. Real net-new value, real net-new spend.

All three roll into one blended NRR.

When it goes up, you get credit for the price clause you never negotiated. When it goes down, you inherit the whole drop, including the expansion the roadmap never gave you anything to sell.

That is the trap.

One number, three jobs, and you only own one of them.

So here is my question: if you decomposed your NRR tomorrow, which slice would actually be yours?

#CustomerSuccess #SaaS #CustomerRetention
Strategy: Pure value. The three-account framework is the most concrete insight in the piece. Ends with a question that forces self-reflection and invites comments.

Content:
The CS leaders who survive the expansion recession will not be the ones who expanded hardest.

They will be the ones who knew exactly which slice of the number was theirs.

Here is how to decompose a blended NRR before anyone decides it for you:

1. The Floor. Gross retention. Are customers staying? If yes, this is healthy and it is yours. Lead with it.

2. The Clause. Growth from contractual price escalators you did not negotiate. Strip it out. It inflates good years and hides bad ones.

3. The Lever. Real net-new expansion. New use cases, new teams, new tiers. This is the only slice that reflects expansion motion.

4. The Lever Gap. The distance between the expansion you ran and the expansion the roadmap actually made possible. Quantify it. This is the ceiling.

When you bring this breakdown, the conversation changes.

In one room I sat in, the CFO went quiet and asked the only question that matters: "So how much of this is actually a pricing decision we haven't made?"

That is the question you want asked. It only gets asked if you bring the decomposition that forces it.

Bring the blended number and you defend a decline for a year. Bring the breakdown and you turn an interrogation into a strategy conversation.

Content:
Owning NRR felt like a win at the time.

It was the number that got Customer Success into the board deck. Proof we drove revenue and not just goodwill. I wanted it. I raised my hand for it.

Then the macro shifted. Upsell stalled. Roadmap stopped shipping things I could sell. CIOs started asking if they even needed to renew at the same level.

And I sat in a room defending a blended number that was falling for reasons no QBR could touch.

The worst part was watching the credit get rewritten. The year it went up, leadership thanked CS, including the price clauses I never negotiated. The year it fell, I owned the whole drop.

Here is what I learned the hard way.

Owning a number you do not control is not ambition. It is exposure.

The job is not to expand harder in a recession that will not let you. The job is to know exactly which slice of the number is yours and say so out loud before anyone decides it for you.

I was the person who did not do that. I am writing so you do not have to be.

Strategy: Vulnerability and a confession. Admitting he volunteered for the trap builds trust and makes the advice land harder than a lecture would.

Content:
Most CS leaders believe they own their platform.

They own a login and a renewal obligation. The platform owns the shape of the team.

Here's the test.

List your last five operational decisions about how your CS team works. How you define a healthy account. How you tier your book. What triggers an intervention. What a CSM does in week one. How you measure success.

Now check each one. Could you change it tomorrow without filing a vendor ticket, waiting on a Professional Services scope, or being told "that's not how the platform handles it"?

Every decision that fails that test isn't yours. It's the vendor's. And you're renting it back from them every year.

The day you adopted your tool, its opinion about how your team should work quietly became your operating model.

You think you bought a system of record. You bought a system of constraint.

Most leaders will renew this quarter without ever noticing. They'll get the 8% discount and the warm feeling of a clean procurement cycle.

And they'll re-sign for another year inside a definition of "healthy" they never wrote.

I broke down the full audit here: https://churnisdead.com/newsletter/cs-platform-org-chart-you-cant-edit

#CustomerSuccess #SaaS #CSLeadership
Strategy: Opens with a counterintuitive reframe (tenancy vs ownership) and a self-applicable test that forces the reader to confront their own dependence. The link sits after the reader is already uncomfortable.

Content:
I call it the Vendor-Default Ratio.

It's the share of your operating model that exists because the tool ships that way, not because you chose it.

You can't read it off a dashboard. But you can feel it the moment someone makes you look.

Think about it.

The vendor's definition of "at-risk" became your definition.

The vendor's idea of a lifecycle stage became your lifecycle.

The fields the tool made easy to filter on silently became your segmentation strategy.

The order of the screens became your CSM workflow.

None of these were decisions. They were defaults. And defaults are decisions someone else made for you, then handed you the bill annually.

The scary part isn't that you inherited them. It's that they became invisible. The tool stopped being an object in the room and became the room itself.

You don't audit the floor you're standing on.

So here's my question for the CS leaders here.

Of the five biggest operational decisions in your function, how many could you actually change tomorrow without a vendor ticket?

Be honest in the comments. My guess is fewer than you'd want to put in writing.

#CustomerSuccess #CSOps #SaaS
Strategy: Pure value, introduces a named concept (Vendor-Default Ratio) that's memorable and shareable, then drives comments with a self-assessment question that's slightly uncomfortable to answer publicly.

Content:
Run this audit before your next renewal.

For each one, ask: could I change this tomorrow without a vendor ticket or a Professional Services scope?

1. How you define a healthy account.
If your health score formula is whatever the platform shipped with, that's not your definition. It's theirs.

2. How you tier your book.
If you tier by the fields the tool made easy to filter, your data model chose your strategy.

3. What triggers an intervention.
If "at-risk" means what the vendor decided at-risk means, your playbook isn't yours.

4. What a CSM does in week one.
If the onboarding follows the order of the screens, the UI designed your motion.

5. How you measure success.
If your reporting is whatever the dashboard exports cleanly, the tool picked your metrics.

Every decision that fails the test isn't renewal. It's re-buying a thousand small choices you never actually made.

That's not renewing a tool. That's renewing an org chart you've never been allowed to edit.

Most leaders will skip this audit and take the 8% discount instead.

Content:
We pulled usage stats. Adoption looked healthy. Finance asked about a cheaper tier. Procurement squeezed out a discount. I signed.

Clean cycle. Felt good.

What I didn't do was ask the only question worth asking. How much of how we worked existed because we chose it, versus because the tool made it the easiest path?

I didn't ask because I'd stopped seeing the tool. It was furniture. The room I worked in, not an object in the room.

You don't audit the floor you're standing on.

It took me embarrassingly long to realize what I'd actually done. I hadn't renewed software. I'd re-signed for another year inside a definition of "healthy" I never wrote and a workflow my CSMs followed because of the order of the screens.

I was managing a team by a number I'd never been allowed to see.

The discomfort of finding out which decisions are actually yours is the point. Most leaders avoid it. I did too, for years.

Content:
Your legal team negotiated a price escalator into that contract. A CPI peg. A fixed annual uplift. An auto-increase tied to renewal.

The customer signed it. Their procurement approved it. And then everyone forgot it existed.

Meanwhile your CS team is grinding an expansion motion for net-new budget in the middle of a CIO spending freeze.

Think about what that means. You are earning the same revenue twice. Once when legal wrote the clause. Again when your CSM tries to manufacture equivalent dollars through a stalled upsell.

The escalator required no approval. No buying committee. No expansion deck. The buyer already agreed to pay.

But at renewal time the CSM was busy pitching a module instead of reading the contract they already had.

I call this the forgotten uplift. The compounding revenue you are contractually owed and have never collected.

It is the cheapest NRR in your portfolio. Nobody posts about it because it is not heroic. There is no champion arc. No victory lap.

Just money quietly evaporating from a PDF nobody has opened since the ink dried.

Go find the money you already own. Then go earn the rest.

#CustomerSuccess #NRR #SaaS
Strategy: Opens with the shocking stat gap, names the concept, and ties it to the current budget freeze pain. Contrarian angle that CS leaders will feel immediately.

Content:
Half of enterprise SaaS contracts carry price-adjustment language. Enforcement runs a fraction of that.

Here is why the clause goes invisible.

One. Low inflation trained everyone to ignore it. For a decade a CPI peg moved the number one or two percent. Not worth the awkward renewal conversation, so teams let it slide. That habit calcified. Then inflation woke up, the clause got real, and the muscle to enforce it had atrophied.

Two. Enforcement feels less strategic than selling. There is no expansion deck for collecting an uplift. No buying committee to map. No LinkedIn victory lap. So CSMs chase the module instead of reading the contract.

Three. Nobody owns the notice date. The window to trigger the increase lapses on a calendar no one is watching. Miss it, and you forfeit the uplift for an entire term.

Every notice window that lapses is money you gave away for a full year. No amount of heroic selling next quarter buys back the year you forfeited by not opening the PDF.

Which of these three is killing your escalator right now?

#CustomerSuccess #RevenueOps #SaaS
Strategy: Pure value breakdown of the mechanism. Question at the end invites self-diagnosis and comment engagement.

Content:
Your enterprise contracts likely contain price escalators you have never enforced. Here is how to go get them.

1. Pull every enterprise contract. Search for the language. CPI peg, fixed uplift, auto-escalator, renewal increase. Flag every one that has it.

2. Find the trigger. Each escalator has a mechanism and a threshold. Know what activates the increase and by how much.

3. Locate the notice date. This is the deadline to enforce. If it is approaching, that is your easiest capture. If you already missed it this term, note the forfeit and calendar the next one now.

4. Build the portfolio number. Recoverable uplift you can still capture, and forfeited uplift dormancy already cost you. Bring both to leadership before anyone sets an expansion target.

5. Stand up the notification play. Every notice date on a calendar the renewals team owns. Enforcement as the default. Holding as the documented exception.

The expansion deck can wait a week. It is stalled on a budget freeze anyway.

The escalator cannot. Every lapsed window is uplift forfeited for a full term.

Content:
For years I bought the story that great CS was about expansion. Champion building. New use cases. The strategic cross-sell into the adjacent business unit.

Collecting a contractual price increase felt small. Administrative. Not the work of someone driving growth.

So I let escalators slide. CPI pegs that would have moved the number a couple points. Not worth the friction, I told myself. Focus on the bigger play.

Then I did the math on a portfolio.

The forgotten uplift across the book was larger than the expansion pipeline the team was killing itself to close. Revenue the buyer had already signed for. Approved by their own procurement. Sitting in PDFs nobody opened.

We had been earning that money twice. Once when legal negotiated it in. Again when we tried to manufacture the equivalent through a stalled upsell against a frozen budget.

Here is what I learned. The heroic motion is not always the profitable one. Sometimes the cheapest NRR in your portfolio is just money you already own.

Go open the PDF before you build the expansion deck.

Content:
Not at the QBR. Not in the save call when the health score flips yellow. Those are theatre.

The decision happens quietly in the first 90 days, when the customer's brain settles on whether they will still be paying you in two years.

Here is the part nobody wants to hear.

Onboarding is the only phase of the lifecycle where CS holds unilateral control. Pricing belongs to the CRO. Expansion drifted to the AE. The roadmap belongs to Product. The customer's politics belong to the customer.

But the depth of the deployment? The number of teams that depend on you? Whether a real outcome gets co-signed by the budget holder? Those are yours. No permission needed.

And what do we do with the only lever we fully own?

We optimise it for speed. We hand the account off as fast as the implementation plan allows. We hit the go-live target and move on.

That is the most expensive mistake in customer success. It is invisible because the damage does not surface for eleven months.

I broke down why onboarding is your only fully controlled renewal, and the metric that actually predicts it, here: https://churnisdead.com/newsletter/onboarding-only-renewal-you-control

#CustomerSuccess #SaaS #Retention
Strategy: Contrarian hot take with a specific time anchor (90 days). Names the control problem clearly and drives to the newsletter with a curiosity gap about the metric.

Content:
Every onboarding metric we love optimises for the vendor's dashboard.

Time to first value. Time to go-live. Time to activation.

What do they all measure? The day the account moves from implementing to live in your CRM and stops consuming your implementation hours.

None of them measure whether the customer built a dependency they cannot easily unwind.

So let me name the number that actually matters. Depth of root.

Did the product get embedded into a workflow the customer cannot rip out? Or did you just deliver a login?

A login is fast. A root is slow.

And every day you shave off implementation to hit a go-live target, you are borrowing against a renewal you will lose in month eleven. When a new VP arrives. When budget review lands. When the one person who understood why they bought you takes another job.

The login churns quietly. The root survives all three.

We measure speed because speed is easy to measure. Depth is hard. So we optimise for the thing that photographs well and lose the thing that keeps the account.

What does your onboarding actually optimise for, honestly? Speed or root?

#CustomerSuccess #Onboarding #ChurnReduction
Strategy: Pure value post attacking a sacred metric (TTV). Introduces the 'depth of root' reframe and ends with a self-audit question to drive comments.

Content:
The accounts that hurt most are not the dramatic churns. They are the quiet ones. Fine accounts. Went live early. Someone was proud of them.

They left because you won the wrong game in the first 90 days.

Five signs you are shipping a login, not growing a root:

1. Success is defined as go-live, not a business outcome co-signed by the budget holder.

2. One team uses the product. You never expanded the footprint during onboarding when you had the momentum.

3. Your only relationship is the champion. When they leave, so does the account.

4. Nobody inside the customer would struggle to remove you. Ripping you out is a Tuesday task, not a project.

5. You measured speed. Time to value, time to activation. Never depth.

Here is the uncomfortable math. Every day shaved off implementation to hit a go-live target is a day borrowed against a renewal you lose in month eleven.

The renewal was never yours to win at renewal. It was yours in month one. Most of us hand it back.

Strategy: Scannable numbered listicle giving immediate diagnostic value. The checklist format invites self-assessment and saves. Link in comments preserves reach.

Content:
Spend a decade in this function and you learn something uncomfortable.

The dramatic churns do not haunt you. The product failures. The pricing fights. The accounts that blew up. You saw those coming. You could explain them.

The ones that hurt are the quiet ones.

The accounts that were fine. That went live early. That someone on the team was genuinely proud of. The ones you moved on from because they looked done.

They did not leave because I failed at the renewal.

They left because I won the wrong game in the first 90 days. I delivered a login when I could have grown a root. Then I chased the next go-live before the account had anything holding it in place.

That took me years to see. Because the damage never shows up when you make the mistake. It surfaces eleven months later, when a new VP arrives or the champion takes another job, and by then the story you tell yourself is that it was out of your hands.

It was in your hands. In month one. You just handed it back.
